params.text:
Managing aggressive or challenging behaviour

When someone becomes openly aggressive, your priority shifts to keeping everyone safe while continuing to de-escalate where you can. Non-physical, non-confrontational approaches are always the first line: they are safer and more effective than trying to overpower or control a person.

  • Speak slowly, softly, and reassuringly — tone matters even when the words are not fully understood.
  • Keep your distance and avoid sudden movements or touch that could be misread as a threat.
  • Address the trigger where you can — ease pain, discomfort, or an unmet need.
  • Avoid raising your voice, standing over the person, or issuing ultimatums.
  • Do not take the behaviour personally; it is usually driven by distress, not by you.

params.alt: A person speaking animatedly during a difficult conversation

params.question: A relative is shouting at you in a corridor, angry that their mother's care visit was late. Which response is most likely to de-escalate the situation?

answers.text: Stay calm, lower your voice, acknowledge their frustration, and invite them to a quieter space to talk it through.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Calm tone, empathy, and offering a private space reduce the audience and the tension while showing you take the concern seriously.

answers.text: Raise your own voice to match theirs so they know you are serious.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Matching their volume escalates the conflict. A calm, quieter tone helps bring the temperature down.

answers.text: Tell them firmly that shouting is unacceptable and walk away immediately.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Dismissing them and walking off can inflame the situation. Withdrawal is for when you feel unsafe, not as a first response to venting.

answers.text: Insist the delay was not your fault and list the reasons it happened.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Defending and arguing turns it into a contest. Acknowledging their feelings first is far more effective than justifying.
